text:
To safeguard the stability and very existence of a state, a legal framework must provide robust regulations for periods of extreme crisis. These "states of emergency" shift the burden of ensuring national security and citizen safety onto state administrative bodies, requiring legal clarity to define the precise scope of executive liability. Fundamentally, such measures are reserved for extraordinary circumstances where standard constitutional mechanisms prove insufficient.

This article examines the evolution of emergency institutions in the Second Polish Republic. By analyzing the March Constitution of 1921, the April Constitution of 1935, and supplementary legal acts of the interwar period, this study explores how the reborn Polish state balanced civil liberties with the necessity of national defense. The analysis highlights how specific legal shifts—most notably the transition toward heightened presidential powers—were driven by the volatile internal and external security landscape of the 1920s and 30s. Utilizing dogmatic-legal and historical-legal methodologies, the article contextualizes the justified application of these extraordinary measures in the face of existential threats to Polish sovereignty.
